cue: How do you say someone did things in the wrong order?

es.text: Empezar la casa por el tejado
es.literal: to start the house at the roof
es.meaning: To do things in the wrong order; to skip the groundwork.
es.example: Compró el coche antes de sacarse el carnet: empezó la casa por el tejado.
es.exampleTranslation: He bought the car before getting his licence — cart before the horse.

it.text: Mettere il carro davanti ai buoi
it.literal: to put the cart in front of the oxen
it.meaning: To do things in the wrong order.
it.example: Ha comprato la macchina prima della patente: ha messo il carro davanti ai buoi.
it.exampleTranslation: He bought the car before the licence — cart before the horse.

bridge.note: Same error, two industries. Spanish builds: a house begun at the roof, with nothing underneath to hold it — a failure of foundations, and the whole thing will fall. Italian hauls: the cart placed in front of the oxen, everything present and correctly assembled but facing the wrong way, so nothing moves. One image says you skipped a step, the other says you reversed one. English keeps the cart and swaps the animal.

interference.es_to_it: Roofs are not the Italian image — the mistake is agricultural: il carro davanti ai BUOI, oxen, not horses.
interference.it_to_es: 'Poner el carro delante de los bueyes' is understood in Spanish but the vivid fixed phrase builds the house from the roof down.

review.notes: ES side discovered in the Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh). IT counterpart from Claude's recall — UNVERIFIED. Foundations-vs-direction framing; the English version sits between the two.
